stimuli.description: Color word whose lexical content matches its ink color (e.g., the word 'red' rendered in red).

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el || !el.style || !el.style.color) return false; const colors = ['red','green','blue','yellow']; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); const color = el.style.color.toLowerCase(); return colors.includes(text) && colors.includes(color) && text === color; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el || !el.style || !el.style.color) return null; const map = {red: 'r', green: 'g', blue: 'b', yellow: 'y'}; const color = el.style.color.toLowerCase(); return map[color] || null; })()

stimuli.description: Color word whose lexical content differs from its ink color (e.g., the word 'red' rendered in blue).

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el || !el.style || !el.style.color) return false; const colors = ['red','green','blue','yellow']; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); const color = el.style.color.toLowerCase(); return colors.includes(text) && colors.includes(color) && text !== color; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el || !el.style || !el.style.color) return null; const map = {red: 'r', green: 'g', blue: 'b', yellow: 'y'}; const color = el.style.color.toLowerCase(); return map[color] || null; })()

phase_detection.test: document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus') !== null && (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus').firstElementChild; if (!el || !el.style) return false; const colors = ['red','green','blue','yellow']; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); return colors.includes(text) && colors.includes((el.style.color || '').toLowerCase()); })()
phase_detection.instructions: (() => { const c = document.querySelector('#jspsych-content'); if (!c) return false; const t = (c.textContent || ''); return t.includes('Welcome to the experiment') || t.includes('Press Space to continue') || t.includes('Press Space to start'); })()

timing.response_window_js: document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus') !== null && (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus').firstElementChild; if (!el || !el.style) return false; const colors = ['red','green','blue','yellow']; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); return colors.includes(text) && colors.includes((el.style.color || '').toLowerCase()); })()
timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return ''; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); const color = (el.style && el.style.color || '').toLowerCase(); return 'word=' + text + ';ink=' + color; })()

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to manual-response Stroop yielded mu near 480-540 ms for congruent (color-congruent) trials in young adults.

citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer type
citations.quote: Young-adult congruent ex-Gaussian mu in the 470-530 ms range for manual-key Stroop.

citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Congruent ex-Gaussian mu estimates ranged approximately 500-560 ms in keypress Stroop variants.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for congruent trials fell in the 40-70 ms range.

citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Congruent sigma centered near 50-75 ms.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau (exponential component) for congruent trials averaged ~90-130 ms.

citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer type
citations.quote: Young-adult congruent tau ~90-140 ms.

congruent.rationale: Ex-Gaussian fits to healthy-adult Stroop color-naming/manual-keypress RTs typically place the Gaussian mode (mu) in the 500-540 ms range with sigma 50-70 ms and tau 90-130 ms for congruent trials. Mean RT (mu + tau) ~630 ms is consistent with web-based 4-choice manual Stroop in typical healthy adults.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent mu shifted modestly relative to congruent (typically <50 ms shift) while tau grew substantially; mu centered ~520-600 ms.

citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Incongruent ex-Gaussian mu estimates between approximately 530-610 ms; conflict effect partitioned into modest mu shift plus larger tau increase.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Manual-response Stroop interference effects on mean RT typically 50-150 ms across studies.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent sigma estimates 50-85 ms.

citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Incongruent sigma ~55-90 ms, modest increase over congruent.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: The Stroop effect concentrated in the right tail: incongruent tau exceeded congruent tau by ~30-60 ms.

citations.title: Distinguishing response conflict and task conflict in the Stroop task: Evidence from ex-Gaussian distribution analysis
citations.quote: Incongruent tau ranged ~120-200 ms across conditions.

citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer type
citations.quote: Tau increased ~30-40 ms from congruent to incongruent in young adults.

incongruent.rationale: Incongruent trials show a Stroop effect concentrated in the right tail of the RT distribution (tau increases substantially more than mu). mu shifts only modestly (~40 ms) while tau grows by ~30-40 ms, producing a mean RT (~705 ms) that exceeds congruent by ~75 ms, within typical Stroop-effect ranges (50-100 ms) for 4-choice manual variants.

citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Choice-RT series exhibit slow trends with positive low-lag autocorrelation consistent with 1/f-class noise; lag-1 ACF values typically 0.08-0.20.

citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Empirical lag-1 autocorrelation across speeded-choice paradigms commonly ~0.10-0.25 after detrending block effects.

autocorrelation.rationale: Weak positive lag-1 autocorrelation in RT (~0.10-0.20) is a generic feature of speeded-choice tasks across paradigms, attributable to slowly drifting attentional/arousal state. Modest value avoids overshooting in a short (15-trial) session.

citations.title: Sustaining attention to simple tasks: A meta-analytic review of the neural mechanisms of vigilant attention
citations.quote: Time-on-task RT slowing in choice-RT paradigms typically emerges only after several hundred trials of sustained performance.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop blocks of standard length (< 100 trials) do not show systematic linear RT inflation attributable to fatigue.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Disabled. The task is only 15 trials in a single block, well below the duration at which linear time-on-task slowing becomes measurable in Stroop (typically requires several hundred trials).

citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Condition repetition effects are not modeled separately from the congruency-sequence (Gratton) interaction; the effect is captured by lag-1 pair structure.

citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Stroop sequence effects are best described as a congruency × previous-congruency interaction, not as a main effect of condition repetition.

condition_repetition.rationale: Disabled. The repetition/alternation structure relevant to conflict tasks is captured more precisely by lag1_pair_modulation (which encodes the specific cC/cI/iC/iI pattern documented in the Stroop literature). Enabling both would double-count the same mechanism.

citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Long-range RT fluctuations contribute ~15-40 ms of within-subject variability beyond i.i.d. noise.

citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: 1/f noise amplitude estimated as 10-50% of total RT variance in speeded-choice tasks.

citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Spectral slopes of RT time series concentrate near alpha = 1 (pink), with empirical estimates 0.6-1.2 across choice-RT tasks.

citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Spectral exponent alpha estimates clustered around 0.7-1.1 for choice-RT data.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f noise in RT series is a robust, paradigm-general feature of human performance and is reported in Stroop and other choice-RT tasks. Modest amplitude (~25 ms SD) reflects within-subject trial-to-trial noise beyond i.i.d. Gaussian variability.

citations.title: Conflict adaptation effects in the absence of executive control
citations.quote: Post-error trials are typically excluded from congruency-sequence analyses to avoid confounding with post-error slowing.

citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Standard practice in the conflict-adaptation literature is to remove the trial following an error before computing the congruency sequence effect.

citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Conflict reduces following conflict: iI is faster than cI by ~25-40 ms, and cC is faster than iC by ~10-20 ms.

citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Across Stroop and related conflict tasks, the congruency sequence interaction is typically 20-50 ms.

citations.title: Anterior cingulate conflict monitoring and adjustments in control
citations.quote: Congruent-following-incongruent (cI) trials slowed by ~15-30 ms relative to congruent-following-congruent (cC); incongruent-following-incongruent (iI) faster than incongruent-following-congruent (iC).

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Conflict-adaptation pattern is robustly documented in Stroop: incongruent-following-incongruent is faster than incongruent-following-congruent (interaction ~25-40 ms), and congruent-following-congruent is faster than congruent-following-incongruent (~10-20 ms). Magnitudes within published ranges for manual Stroop. skip_after_error=true because post-error trials are confounded with post-error slowing and are typically excluded from CSE analyses.

citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Reaction time on the trial following an error is consistently slowed by tens of milliseconds across choice-RT paradigms.

citations.title: Post-error slowing: an orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes of ~25-60 ms in standard choice-RT tasks including Stroop.

authors: van der Maas, H. L. J.
citations.title: Testing theories of post-error slowing
citations.quote: PES magnitudes typically 20-60 ms across choice-RT tasks; effect concentrated on the immediately-following trial.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is well documented in Stroop and conflict tasks generally, with typical magnitudes of 25-55 ms on the trial immediately following an error. Range chosen to cover central published estimates for healthy adults on speeded choice tasks.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Practice effects on Stroop RT are modest at short block lengths (<30 trials) and are typically estimated only with longer training schedules.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Asymptote of practice-related RT reduction is not reliably estimable from single-block, short-duration Stroop tasks.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Block size is a design parameter, not a literature-derived quantity; reported Stroop blocks range from ~15 to several hundred trials.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Practice-decay parameters cannot be reliably estimated from a 15-trial single-block session.

practice_effect.rationale: Disabled. The task is 15 trials with no separate practice block; published practice/warmup effects in Stroop are typically modest and absorbed by trial-to-trial noise at this trial count. Enabling would require a longer session to estimate the decay reliably.

citations.title: Sustaining attention to simple tasks: A meta-analytic review of the neural mechanisms of vigilant attention
citations.quote: Vigilance decrements in choice-RT tasks become measurable over task durations exceeding ~15-30 minutes; short blocks (<5 minutes) show negligible decrement.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Disabled. Vigilance/sustained-attention decrements emerge over much longer task durations than 15 trials and are not documented as a meaningful contributor to RT variance in short Stroop blocks.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of overall mean RT in healthy-adult Stroop is ~70-100 ms; Stroop-effect magnitude varies by ~20-35 ms SD across subjects. Sigma/tau multiplicative jitter of 0.75-1.3 accommodates published between-subject ranges in ex-Gaussian parameters. Accuracy is high and tightly clustered in healthy adults on the 4-choice manual Stroop (~95-99%); SD ~0.02 and a clip floor of 0.85 prevent unrealistic tail draws. Omission rate is very low and similarly clipped.

citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean RT in healthy-adult Stroop reported as ~70-110 ms across samples.

citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer type
citations.quote: Young-adult mean RT SD ~75-95 ms across subjects in manual Stroop.

citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Stroop-effect magnitude (incongruent − congruent) varies across subjects with SD ~20-35 ms.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop interference effects across studies show substantial between-sample variability consistent with subject-level SD ~25-35 ms.

citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Across subjects, ex-Gaussian sigma and tau estimates spanned approximately 0.7-1.4× the group mean.

citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer type
citations.quote: Individual sigma/tau estimates vary by roughly 0.75-1.3× the sample mean in young adults.

citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Accuracy in healthy-adult Stroop tightly clustered (~95-99%); between-subject SD ~0.015-0.030.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Accuracy in standard manual Stroop is uniformly high across healthy adults.

citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission/no-response trials in unspeeded healthy-adult Stroop typically <1%, with low between-subject variability.

citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Healthy-adult Stroop accuracy reliably above 85% on incongruent trials and near ceiling on congruent.

citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Subject-level Stroop accuracy 0.85-1.00 with vast majority >0.92.

citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission rates above ~5% are uncommon and typically flagged for exclusion in standard Stroop datasets.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'Stroop Online (cognition.run)' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jspsych.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-instructions.js, runner-D7ZihNOY.js, jspsych.css, styles.css, inline_script_1.js, inline_script_2.js.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 4 temporal effects.
Produced 50 citations across 27 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 33 parameters; 6 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed first attempt: 20 trials matched, conditions ['incongruent'] all observed.
